claim: The Currier A/B split represents two different scribes encoding the same underlying Latin text using the same cipher but with different personal orthographic conventions â€” specifically, Scribe A preferentially uses one set of homophone variants while Scribe B uses a complementary set, causing the apparent 'dialect' difference to be a cipher-level artifact rather than a linguistic one.
evidence_for: Currier B (23,766 words) is 2.16x larger than Currier A (11,022 words), consistent with one dominant scribe and one secondary scribe
Multiple current beliefs support same-language/different-encoding interpretation (0.677, 0.214, 0.101)
If homophones exist, different scribes selecting different homophone variants would produce surface-level vocabulary differences without underlying linguistic difference
Medieval scribal practice commonly involved multiple hands on the same manuscript using shared but slightly varied notation
evidence_against: Belief H004 (0.837) and H008 (0.781) strongly support genuine linguistic difference between A and B
If same cipher with homophone variant selection, the glyph frequency distributions between A and B sections should be mirror-complementary â€” this has not been confirmed
The magnitude of A/B vocabulary divergence may be too large to explain by homophone variant preference alone
test: Compute glyph unigram frequency distributions separately for Currier A and Currier B sections. For each of the 25 glyphs, compute the A/B frequency ratio. If orthographic homophone variant swapping explains A/B, expect to find glyph pairs (g1, g2) where g1 is overrepresented in A and g2 is overrepresented in B in a complementary pattern. Also compute the overlap coefficient of A and B vocabulary (words appearing in both vs. unique to each).
test_metric: Number of glyph pairs showing complementary A/B frequency inversion (ratio >2.0 in one direction); vocabulary overlap coefficient between A and B sections
test_threshold: Confirm if â‰¥4 complementary glyph pairs are identified AND vocabulary overlap coefficient >0.45; deny if <2 complementary pairs exist or overlap coefficient <0.25

reasoning: Distinguishing whether the A/B split is linguistic or scribal is critical: if scribal, the entire corpus can be treated as encoding one language, dramatically simplifying decipherment.
